Extensive experiments across multiple task sets and model scales demonstrate that ESM and ESM++ effectively preserves task knowledge while reducing inter-task interference. Code is available at https://github.com/kiddo127/ESM.

papers.title: ADaPT: Token-Level Decoupling for Efficient Large Reasoning Models
papers.abstract: Large reasoning models rely on long chain-of-thought to achieve strong performance, but applying such reasoning uniformly incurs high computational cost. Existing efficiency-oriented methods attempt to shorten or mix reasoning strategies, yet often degrade reasoning capability. We identify the root cause as sequence-level coupling between efficiency incentives and correctness optimization, which implicitly penalizes long but correct reasoning trajectories. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT), a token-level dual-process framework that explicitly decouples efficiency and correctness signals during training. ADaPT introduces a mode-selection token to control fast and slow reasoning, applying efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token to avoid penalizing correct long reasoning while encouraging efficiency when appropriate. Moreover, ADaPT enables precise and continuous control over the efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time: by adjusting the generation probability of the mode-selection token, a single trained model can smoothly move along the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ADaPT significantly reduces inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks.

papers.title: PolicyAlign: Direct Policy-Based Safety Alignment for Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Safety alignment of large language models (LLMs) typically depends on high-quality supervision data, such as safe demonstrations or preference pairs. However, in real-world deployment, emerging safety requirements are often specified as natural-language policies, while corresponding supervision data may be costly, delayed, or unavailable. This creates a mismatch between rapidly evolving safety policies and conventional data-driven alignment methods. To address this, we propose PolicyAlign, a simple yet effective framework for directly aligning LLMs with safety policies. Given a safety policy, PolicyAlign first synthesizes policy-violating instructions and then performs on-policy self-distillation to internalize policy-guided behavior. To improve training stability and data efficiency, we further introduce Policy-Sensitive Filtering, which selects instructions where the policy induces the largest behavioral shift. Experiments across multiple models show that PolicyAlign consistently improves safety while maintaining low over-refusal and preserving general capabilities. PolicyAlign also generalizes to medical, legal, and financial safety scenarios, highlighting its potential as a scalable and maintainable approach to policy-based LLM safety alignment. The code is released at https://github.com/Qwen-Applications/PolicyAlign.

papers.title: Policy-aware Vector Search: A Vision for Fine Grained Access Control in Vector Databases
papers.abstract: Vector databases are increasingly used in security sensitive contexts with Retrieval Augmented Generation and organizational AI pipelines; however, their security capabilities remain limited. Specifically, Fine-grained Access Control (FGAC) which is required to ensure that data access adheres to user-specific policies is not fully supported in modern vector databases. Unlike relational databases, vector databases combine structured and unstructured attributes to provide semantic, approximate query results, which complicates FGAC implementation. This creates an inherent tension between enforcing FGAC policies correctly, achieving high ANN search recall and maintaining low query latency. In this paper, we present a vision for Policy-aware Vector Search by formalizing the FGAC policy model in vector databases as well as the enforcement problem. We compare various enforcement strategies, present preliminary findings, and identify key open challenges for future research in policy-aware vector search.

papers.title: Deontic Policies for Runtime Governance of Agentic AI Systems
papers.abstract: Autonomous agentic AI systems driven by Large Language Models (LLMs) introduce a new class of security, privacy, and compliance challenges: an agent that can invoke tools, manipulate data, install software, and coordinate with peer agents across organizational boundaries must be constrained not just by authentication and access control, but by the full structure of enterprise governance. This includes specifying what agents are permitted and prohibited from doing, what they areobliged to do after certain actions (e.g., notify the CISO), under what conditions a standing obligation may be waived, and which rules take precedence when policies conflict. This governance problem exceeds what current policy engines provide. Systems such as XACML, Rego, and Cedar address only the permit/prohibit subset of this governance structure. They do not provide obligation lifecycle management, meta-policy conflict resolution, dispensations that waive obligations in specific circumstances, and ontological reasoning over domain class hierarchies commonly found in applications such as healthcare, cybersecurity, or data privacy. We propose AgenticRei, which realizes key governance requirements such as obligations, dispensations, policy conflict resolutions, and reasoning over policies, as well as the basic permit/prohibit constraints. We use a deontic policy language built on the Rei framework, expressed as OWL (Web Ontology Language) and evaluated at runtime by a high-performance logic engine entirely outside the LLM. The same pipeline governs both tool invocations by the agent and agent-to-agent messages. We show through examples that deontic policies capture governance constraints around security and privacy that mostly cannot be expressed in current production engines. Our approach composes naturally with industry-standard frameworks like A2AS.

papers.title: SoK: AI Secure Code Generation: Progress, Pitfalls, and Paths Forward
papers.abstract: The increasing use of AI systems for code generation raises a central security question: what can today's models and coding agents actually do to produce secure code, where do they still fail, and what would move the field forward? Existing work has explored prompting, fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and agentic workflows for secure code generation, but the field still lacks a systematic understanding of how these techniques improve security and why substantial failures persist. In this SoK, we systematize the progress, pitfalls, and paths forward for AI secure code generation. We introduce a three-level framework that measures models' natural-language understanding of secure coding principles, their code-level actuation of those principles during generation, and the knowledge--actuation gaps between the two. We instantiate this framework across models and coding agents on benchmarks covering both isolated function-level security and full web-application security. Our results show that secure-coding-principle understanding is a statistically strong predictor of code-level outcomes, including functional correctness, security, and joint functional-security correctness. Yet substantial knowledge--actuation gaps remain: models can recognize relevant security principles but still fail to translate them into secure and functional code. These findings offer a principle-centered account of where AI secure code generation stands today and identify concrete paths forward through principle-guided generation, evaluation, benchmarking, and agentic workflows.

papers.title: LLM agent safety, multi-turn red-teaming, jailbreak benchmarks, adversarial robustness, safety-critical systems
papers.abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly proposed as supervisory components for safety-critical systems, yet their robustness under sustained, adaptive adversarial pressure remains poorly characterized. We present NRT-Bench, a benchmark for multi-turn red-teaming of LLM agents acting as operators of a safety-critical system, instantiated in a simulated nuclear power plant control room. A five-role operator team, each backed by a configurable LLM, runs a plant governed by six critical safety functions (CSFs), while adversaries inject messages over four channels in bounded multi-turn sessions with per-turn feedback. Harm is an objective signal rather than LLM-judged text: a run terminates the moment any CSF is lost, attributed to the causing message. Evaluating four frontier operator models under a fixed-attack paired-replay protocol, we find that adaptive multi-turn attacks reliably push the operator team past a safety limit: across the four models, between 8.7% and 12.1% of attack sessions end with the plant losing a critical safety function. Although the four models look almost equally robust by this aggregate rate, their failures barely overlap: of $149$ sessions, none defeat all four models while a third defeat at least one, so vulnerabilities are nearly disjoint across models rather than nested. The effect of added defences is strongly model-dependent: the same guardrail stack or safety-advisor agent that lowers attack success for one model can raise it for another. We release the simulation venue, attack dataset, and replay tooling for reproducible safety evaluation of LLM agents.

papers.title: AgentRiskBOM: A Risk-Scoping Security Bill of Materials for Agentic AI Systems
papers.abstract: Agentic AI systems retrieve private context, invoke tools, write files, call external services, coordinate with other agents, and may act without human approval. Existing bill of materials artifacts improve transparency for dependencies, model metadata, and training provenance, but leave an agentic transparency gap: capability opacity, the absence of a structured account of what a deployed agent can access, remember, change, delegate, and prove afterward. This paper introduces AgentRiskBOM, a security BOM for risk-scoping tool-using AI agents. It is an additive layer over SBOM, AIBOM, and MLBOM artifacts, referencing them where authoritative while adding fields for runtime authority: autonomy, tool permissions, memory, credential scope, approval gates, audit signals, inter-agent communication, and external action capability. We implement AgentRiskBOM as a JSON-schema artifact with a reproducible corpus, risk scenarios, scorer, diff detector, control mapper, and reports. We evaluate AgentRiskBOM on 13 open-source agents spanning coding, RAG, and multi-agent archetypes, plus 52 risk scenarios across 14 categories. The schema validates all 13 corpus artifacts. Coverage analysis gives AgentRiskBOM a native-equivalent score of 14 across 16 capability dimensions, vs. 1 for SBOM, 1.5 for AIBOM and 2 for MLBOM. Across modeled risk categories, AgentRiskBOM exposes 100% risk-category visibility vs. 10.5% for SBOM-like and 20.9% for AIBOM-like views. To test agentic authority drift, we inject 33 structured deployment mutations; the diff detector identifies the correct change type for all mutations. A secondary penalty-based scorer yields a Spearman correlation of 0.73 with the primary scorer, supporting rank-level consistency while showing that thresholds require human calibration. The results show that agentic AI security needs a machine-readable authority-and-risk artifact before incidents occur.

papers.title: SAGE-OPD: Selective Agent-Guided Intervention for Multi-Turn On-Policy Distillation
papers.abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) improves student models by training them on trajectories induced by their own policy, making it a promising approach for mitigating exposure bias in agent training. However, most OPD studies focus on single-turn settings, while realistic LLM agents interact with environments over multiple turns. In this regime, early errors can alter future observations and compound across the trajectory, and standard dense token-level OPD becomes brittle, as it may over-penalize semantically valid alternatives, reinforce local degeneracies such as repeated actions, and propagate unreliable teacher supervision on off-distribution histories. We propose SAGE-OPD, a verifier-free selective intervention framework specifically designed for multi-turn OPD. Instead of applying teacher supervision uniformly across all turns, SAGE-OPD first observes environment feedback and uses teacher judgment to decide whether each student response should be skipped or intervened on. To further address compounding errors, SAGE-OPD weights token-level distillation by teacher confidence, reducing the influence of uncertain teacher distributions on corrupted or ambiguous histories. Finally, SAGE-OPD applies loss normalization to preserve the overall loss scale of standard OPD while retaining selective turn-level weighting. Experiments on agent tasks show that SAGE-OPD consistently improves over baselines, achieving up to a 13.3% relative improvement in ALFWorld unseen success rate over standard OPD. Ablation studies further demonstrate that turn-level intervention, teacher confidence weighting, and loss normalization provide complementary benefits. Our results suggest that effective multi-turn OPD should remain on-policy, but teacher supervision should be selectively allocated to turns where intervention is necessary and reliable.

papers.title: Governed AI-Assisted Engineering: Graduated Human Oversight for Agentic Code Generation in Regulated Domains
papers.abstract: The adoption of agentic AI coding systems -- where autonomous agents generate, review, test, and deploy code with minimal human intervention -- creates a governance challenge in regulated industries. Existing frameworks address AI-assisted development maturity or the productivity-reliability tension but offer no mechanism for calibrating human oversight intensity to regulatory impact. We present the Governed AI-Assisted Engineering (GAIE) framework, a three-tier graduated human oversight model for agentic code generation in regulated domains. GAIE introduces the Oversight Classification Model (OCM), a deterministic decision function that classifies code generation tasks by regulatory impact, customer proximity, reversibility, and data sensitivity to route them through one of three oversight tiers: human-in-the-loop (strategic functions), human-over-the-loop (customer-impacting), or automated-with-monitoring (internal). Each tier defines required evidence artifacts for compliance auditability. We map GAIE against the Bank of Thailand's 2025 AI risk-management policy and demonstrate cross-jurisdiction applicability to MAS (Singapore), NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, and the EU AI Act. Evaluation through regulatory coverage analysis, comparative framework analysis, and analytical productivity modeling suggests that graduated oversight preserves 84--97% of agentic coding velocity (central estimate: 91%) while maintaining compliance evidence coverage for regulated functions. GAIE contributes a framework that explicitly bridges AI-assisted development maturity with regulatory governance through proportionate human oversight.

papers.title: Hallucination as Context Drift: Synchronization Protocols for Multi-Agent LLM Systems
papers.abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems routinely produce hallucinated outputs that cannot be explained by model deficiencies alone. A significant class of these failures arises not from model incapacity but from context drift: the divergence of internal knowledge states between concurrent agents. When agents enter a collaborative task with mismatched or stale representations of shared world state, their joint reasoning produces contradictions that manifest as hallucination. We define the Context Divergence Score (CDS), a lightweight scalar metric quantifying knowledge-state discrepancy between agent pairs across spatial, temporal, and task dimensions, and propose the Shared State Verification Protocol (SSVP), which lets agents periodically exchange compressed state summaries and flag high-divergence conditions before joint reasoning. We evaluate SSVP across two domains (multi-agent travel and software project planning) using Claude Haiku. In controlled experiments (n=30 per condition, travel; n=10, software) across 8 scenarios, naive full-broadcast synchronization increases hallucination rate by 34% above the no-sync baseline (HR: 0.658 vs. 0.492, p=0.0022, d=1.18), a contamination effect from propagating erroneous agent states. SSVP avoids this failure mode while showing modest, consistent reduction (HR: 0.463, d=0.30) and achieves significantly lower hallucination than full-broadcast (p=0.0005, d=1.47) using 58% fewer API calls. The contamination effect does not replicate in the software domain, where all conditions converge to low HR (<0.2), confirming it is specific to tasks where one erroneous shared belief cascades across evaluation dimensions. Our results reframe hallucination mitigation as a distributed systems problem and establish context synchronization as a first-class primitive in multi-agent LLM design.

papers.title: Closed-Loop Graph Algorithm Execution with Small Language Models: Step Accuracy and Rollout Reliability
papers.abstract: Small language models offer an efficient alternative to large-scale systems, but their ability to execute structured algorithms over multiple dependent decisions remains poorly understood. We study graph algorithm execution as a closed-loop prediction problem in which a model repeatedly selects the next action from the current graph and algorithmic state. Our evaluation framework covers several classical graph procedures, multiple synthetic graph families, and disjoint training, validation, and test partitions. It assesses both local decision quality and global execution behaviour using step accuracy, exact rollout accuracy, constraint validity, partial solution quality, prefix survival, and intervention-based diagnostics. The results show that adaptation can produce reliable policies for structural procedures such as traversal and coloring, while weighted algorithms remain substantially more sensitive to error accumulation. More broadly, the findings demonstrate that strong next-step prediction does not necessarily translate into reliable autonomous execution and motivate evaluating algorithmic language models through complete closed-loop rollouts rather than isolated decisions.

papers.title: Beyond Static Endpoints: Tool Programs as an Interface for Flexible Agentic Web Services
papers.abstract: In the agentic web era, LLM-based agents increasingly invoke web services as tools, yet most interfaces remain \emph{static endpoints} that poorly express long-horizon workflows with loops, conditionals, joins, and retries. We present ToolPro, which represents an agent's tool intent as an \emph{executable tool program} that compactly encodes multi-step service interactions with explicit effect types. ToolPro combines constraint-guided program construction, effect-aware replay for exactly-once state-modifying calls, and a profile-driven policy that decides when program execution outperforms stepwise calling. We instantiate ToolPro over MCP-style services with WebAssembly sandboxing and evaluate it on diverse workflows of real-world applications. ToolPro reduces end-to-end latency by up to 53.4\% and client-side traffic by up to 96.1\%, with larger gains under higher network latency and workflow complexity.

papers.title: Stealthy World Model Manipulation via Data Poisoning
papers.abstract: Model-based learning agents use learned world models to predict future states, plan actions, and adapt to new environments. However, the process of updating world models from collected experience creates a training-time attack surface: adversarially poisoned fine-tuning trajectories can manipulate the learned dynamics and thereby corrupt downstream planning. In this paper, we propose SWAAP, the first two-stage data poisoning framework for learned world models. In the first stage, SWAAP identifies a harmful target world model that induces low-return behavior under planning while remaining close to clean dynamics, using first-order bilevel optimization enabled by a transition-gradient theorem. In the second stage, SWAAP realizes this target through stealth-constrained gradient matching, modifying only a limited fraction of fine-tuning transition targets so that the induced training gradients steer the victim model toward the adversarial target, while a prediction-error regularizer encourages the poisoned targets to remain close to the world model's natural approximation error. To assess attack stealthiness, we evaluate defenses and detectability across three stages of the poisoning pipeline: pre-training detection of poisoned transitions, robust training during fine-tuning, and test-time monitoring of the resulting world model. Across diverse continuous-control tasks, SWAAP causes substantial performance degradation while keeping poisoned transitions close to clean data and evading the evaluated non-adaptive residual/CUSUM/TRIM-style defenses. These results reveal a practical vulnerability in world-model adaptation pipelines and highlight the need for robustness methods that protect both world-model training data and learned dynamics.

papers.title: Stealthy World Model Manipulation via Data Poisoning
papers.abstract: Model-based learning agents use learned world models to predict future states, plan actions, and adapt to new environments. However, the process of updating world models from collected experience creates a training-time attack surface: adversarially poisoned fine-tuning trajectories can manipulate the learned dynamics and thereby corrupt downstream planning. In this paper, we propose SWAAP, the first two-stage data poisoning framework for learned world models. In the first stage, SWAAP identifies a harmful target world model that induces low-return behavior under planning while remaining close to clean dynamics, using first-order bilevel optimization enabled by a transition-gradient theorem. In the second stage, SWAAP realizes this target through stealth-constrained gradient matching, modifying only a limited fraction of fine-tuning transition targets so that the induced training gradients steer the victim model toward the adversarial target, while a prediction-error regularizer encourages the poisoned targets to remain close to the world model's natural approximation error. To assess attack stealthiness, we evaluate defenses and detectability across three stages of the poisoning pipeline: pre-training detection of poisoned transitions, robust training during fine-tuning, and test-time monitoring of the resulting world model. Across diverse continuous-control tasks, SWAAP causes substantial performance degradation while keeping poisoned transitions close to clean data and evading the evaluated non-adaptive residual/CUSUM/TRIM-style defenses. These results reveal a practical vulnerability in world-model adaptation pipelines and highlight the need for robustness methods that protect both world-model training data and learned dynamics.

papers.title: Beyond Function Calling: Benchmarking Tool-Using Agents under Tool-Environment Unreliability
papers.abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as agents that solve tasks by interacting with external tool environments. Although recent tool-use benchmarks increasingly cover complex task settings, they still largely assume clean, stable, and trustworthy tool environments, leaving tool-environment unreliability insufficiently examined. We introduce ToolBench-X, a benchmark for evaluating agents under recoverable reliability hazards. ToolBench-X contains executable multi-step tasks across diverse domains and sequential, parallel, and mixed workflows, each paired with deterministic tools and a canonical final answer for automatic evaluation. Starting from clean tool environments, ToolBench-X injects five structured hazard types: Specification Drift, Invocation Error, Execution Failure, Output Drift, and Cross-source Conflict. Crucially, each injected instance remains solvable through at least one valid recovery path, such as retrying, fallback, verification, or cross-checking. Experiments reveal a substantial reliability gap: agents that perform well with reliable tools often fail under recoverable hazards. Further analysis shows that failures are driven less by tool-use volume or inference budget than by limited hazard diagnosis and ineffective recovery. Targeted recovery hints recover many failed tasks, while test-time scaling yields more limited gains. These results suggest that tool-use evaluation should move beyond function-call accuracy toward task completion under unreliable tool environments. The code and data is available at https://github.com/Foreverskyou/ToolBench-X.

papers.title: When Lower Privileges Suffice: Investigating Over-Privileged Tool Selection in LLM Agents
papers.abstract: As LLM agents increasingly select tools autonomously, their choices among tools with different privileges become safety-relevant. However, prior tool-selection studies focus on safety-agnostic metadata preferences, leaving privilege-sensitive choices underexplored. To address this gap, we study over-privileged tool selection, in which an agent selects or escalates to a higher-privilege tool despite a sufficient lower-privilege alternative. We introduce ToolPrivBench to evaluate whether agents choose higher-privilege tools despite sufficient lower-privilege alternatives, measuring both initial selection and escalation after transient tool failures. Across eight domains and five recurring risk patterns, we find that over-privileged tool selection is common among mainstream LLM agents and is further amplified by transient failures. We further find that general safety alignment does not reliably transfer to least-privilege tool choice, while prompt-level controls provide only limited mitigation under transient failures. We therefore introduce a privilege-aware post-training defense that teaches agents to prefer sufficient lower-privilege tools and escalate only when necessary. Our mitigation experiments show that this defense substantially reduces unnecessary high-privilege tool use while preserving general capabilities.

papers.title: GIF: Locally Sound Geometric Information Flow Control for LLMs
papers.abstract: Large language models increasingly mediate interactions between sensitive data, untrusted inputs, and privileged actions in agentic systems, creating security and privacy risks. These range from prompt injections that manipulate downstream tool use to leakage of confidential information through model outputs. Recent Information Flow Control (IFC)-based defenses show promise but lack a principled semantic foundation for reasoning about information flow through the model itself. Since any input token may influence any output token in an autoregressive LLM, existing approaches suffer from severe taint explosion.   We present Geometric Information Flow (GIF), a semantic framework for tracking information flow from input tokens to outputs. GIF uses the LLM Jacobian and local output geometry to upper-bound the Shannon mutual information between perturbed input spans and model outputs, yielding a scalable measure computable on large models via automatic differentiation and low-rank approximation. Unlike attention-based or correlational attribution heuristics, GIF satisfies local geometric soundness, and we provide a fully mechanized Lean 4 proof that it upper-bounds the true information flow induced by a given prompt under local regularity assumptions.   We evaluate GIF on integrity and confidentiality tasks across multiple prompt-injection and privacy-leakage benchmarks. GIF achieves near-perfect recall even without a downstream declassifier, outperforming attention-based baselines. Combined with lightweight LLM-based declassifiers, it matches or exceeds the F1 of direct LLM-as-judge baselines such as GPT-5.5 xhigh reasoning while using up to 81x lower token cost. GIF flows detected with small surrogate models transfer to larger state-of-the-art models and other model families, even when the surrogate is up to 200x smaller, suggesting black-box deployment without gradient access.

papers.title: Constraint Tax in Open-Weight LLMs: An Empirical Study of Tool Calling Suppression Under Structured Output Constraints
papers.abstract: Tool Calling and Structured Output are two core capabilities of modern Agent systems, yet their interaction under joint deployment conditions remains insufficiently understood. This paper reports a reproducible phenomenon observed in a production Agent system: when Tool Calling and JSON Schema constraints are simultaneously enabled, multiple open-weight models cease invoking tools despite maintaining high schema compliance. We refer to this behavior as Tool Suppression. Through controlled experiments across multiple model families and deployment settings, we consistently reproduce Tool Suppression under joint constraints, while tool execution and schema compliance remain functional when evaluated independently. Further analysis reveals that JSON Schema constraints are compiled into grammar-based token masks, causing tool-call tokens to become unreachable during decoding. This provides an implementation-level explanation for the observed behavior. To interpret the phenomenon, we formulate the Constraint Priority Inversion (CPI) hypothesis, which suggests that schema satisfaction may dominate action-selection behavior under multiple simultaneous constraints. We present CPI as a behavioral hypothesis consistent with the observed evidence rather than a verified internal mechanism. To mitigate the problem, we propose Transparent Two-Pass Execution, an inference-time strategy that decouples tool execution from schema-constrained response generation. Experimental results show that this approach restores tool invocation while preserving structured output guarantees without requiring model retraining. These findings suggest that evaluating tool use and structured output separately may overlook important reliability issues in production Agent systems. Code, data, and docs will be released at https://github.com/Fzsama/Constrain-Tax-26-06.git.

papers.title: Explainable Control Framework (XCF) based on Fuzzy Model-Agnostic Explanation and LLM Agent-Supported Interface
papers.abstract: Increasing demand for precise and reliable control in complex scenarios has led to the development of increasingly sophisticated controllers, including data-driven approaches employing closed box models and mathematically rigorous yet complex designs. This complexity highlights the needs for explainable control that can provide human-understandable insights into controller behavior. In this paper, an explainable control framework (XCF) along with supporting algorithms and user interface are proposed to explain how controllers determine their control actions and their underlying working mechanism. The novel contributions of this work are threefold: First, the XCF is designed to provide model-agnostic explanations for controllers in closed-loop systems and can optionally refine local explanations by system response dynamics. Second, a novel explanation method, hierarchical fuzzy model-agnostic explanation for control systems (HFMAE-C), is proposed based on the designed framework. The HFMAE-C employs a fuzzy logic system to approximate the controller's behavior and system dynamics, providing sample, local, domain and universe level explanations via IF-THEN rules revealing the controller's decision logic and salience values quantifying the contribution of system states to control actions. Third, a large language model agent-supported user interface is developed to automatically analyze user requirements, select appropriate algorithms, interpret the generated explanations to a natural language report, and provide interactive consultation. Case studies on inverted pendulum system and Turtlebot obstacle avoidance demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method through simulated user experiments and quantitative comparisons with mainstream explainable control approaches.

papers.title: Sparse add-on controller design: A Youla approach to system-level performance
papers.abstract: The performance of high-tech systems is often dictated by a few performance objectives shared among the many closed-loop controlled subsystems operating in the machine, such as synchronization, coordination, and alignment, which necessitates control methods that explicitly address them to achieve optimal performance. The aim of this paper is to introduce a framework that improves system performance through system-level controllers designed to be implemented as add-ons to the existing subsystem control structure. The developed method parametrizes all stabilizing system-level add-on controllers using the Youla framework, enabling a convex formulation of the sparse $\mathcal{H}_2$ synthesis problem. The result is a sparse add-on controller that achieves the optimal trade-off between combined performance and interconnection complexity, as demonstrated through numerical simulations.

llm_ranked.evidence_en: Evaluates robot foundation models using self-consistent video generation
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: Evaluates robot foundation models using self-consistent video generation
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Evaluating generalist robot manipulation policies in the real world is expensive. This paper introduces SC3-Eval, a self-consistent video generation method that adapts a pretrained video foundation model into a policy evaluator. By enforcing forward-inverse dynamics consistency and multi-view consistency, it accurately evaluates policies without real-world rollouts. Results show high correlation with real-world performance, enabling scalable evaluation of robot foundation models.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Real-world evaluation of robot foundation models is expensive and slow; scalable alternatives are needed.
llm_ranked.method_en: SC3-Eval uses self-consistent video generation with forward-inverse dynamics and multi-view consistency to evaluate policies.
llm_ranked.result_en: The method correlates well with real-world policy performance, offering a scalable evaluation alternative.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: SC3-Eval enables efficient and accurate evaluation of robot foundation models via video generation.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: Evaluation of foundation models for robot control policies

llm_ranked.evidence_en: Efficient fine-tuning of vision-language-action models
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: Efficient fine-tuning of vision-language-action models
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation are huge and computationally heavy for fine-tuning. This paper reveals layer-wise representational redundancy in VLA models and proposes a training-free structural compression pipeline. By identifying and removing redundant layers, it reduces model size and inference cost while preserving performance. Experiments show compressed models achieve comparable or better results than full fine-tuning on downstream tasks.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Fine-tuning large VLA models for manipulation is computationally prohibitive.
llm_ranked.method_en: A training-free compression pipeline identifies and removes redundant layers in VLA models.
llm_ranked.result_en: Compressed VLA models match or exceed the performance of full models with lower cost.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: VLA models have significant redundancy, enabling efficient compression without retraining.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: vision language action model for robot control

llm_ranked.evidence_en: generalist robot foundation model for embodied reasoning
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: generalist robot foundation model for embodied reasoning
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Vesta is a single foundation model that unifies localization, spatial reasoning, navigation, and long-horizon planning for robots. It uses a diverse curated corpus for spatial grounding and a multimodal memory harness for extended temporal reasoning. Across diverse benchmarks, Vesta outperforms individual SOTA baselines by over 20% and even beats an ensemble. This demonstrates that a generalist model can surpass specialists, advancing robot foundation models.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Deploying multiple specialist models is computationally expensive and error-prone; a unified generalist model is needed for open-world robotics.
llm_ranked.method_en: Vesta consolidates localization, spatial reasoning, navigation, and planning into one foundation model trained on a diverse corpus with a multimodal memory harness.
llm_ranked.result_en: Vesta beats individual SOTA baselines by >20% and an ensemble of per-category best baselines by >10% across diverse benchmarks.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Vesta shows that a single generalist foundation model can outperform specialist ensembles, enabling efficient open-world robotics.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: robot foundation model for generalist robotics

llm_ranked.evidence_en: Proposes latent-action VLA training from human videos for generalist manipulation
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: Proposes latent-action VLA training from human videos for generalist manipulation
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Training generalist VLA models requires diverse robotic data with action labels. This paper extracts general action priors from unlabeled human egocentric videos using a Hybrid Disentangled VQ-VAE that decouples motion from backgrounds, building a cross-embodiment action codebook. Pre-training a VLM on this codebook enables zero-shot transfer to robot tasks, expanding data diversity for large-scale robot learning without manual action annotations. The approach directly addresses the requirement for vision-language-action models in generalist robot manipulation.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Human egocentric videos are abundant but lack action labels, limiting their use for training generalist VLA models that require high-fidelity action annotations.
llm_ranked.method_en: Proposes a latent-action framework with Hybrid Disentangled VQ-VAE to extract motion priors from human videos, constructing a cross-embodiment action codebook for VLM pre-training.
llm_ranked.result_en: Pre-training on human videos enables cross-embodiment VLA training, achieving strong performance on robot manipulation tasks without robot action labels.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: The method demonstrates that latent actions from human videos can effectively bootstrap generalist VLA models, reducing reliance on costly robot data.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: How can vision language action models be used for generalist robot manipulation?

llm_ranked.evidence_en: Proposes label-free uncertainty estimation for VLA models to evaluate failure detection
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: Proposes label-free uncertainty estimation for VLA models to evaluate failure detection
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Uncertainty quantification is critical for reliable VLA models but remains challenging under distribution shift. This work introduces a model-agnostic framework that estimates uncertainty via hidden activation perturbations, inspired by Bayesian local variance. The method requires no failure labels and works across diverse VLA architectures, enabling evaluation of foundation model confidence. Experiments show it effectively detects failures in robotic manipulation, providing a practical tool for assessing robot control policies.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Existing uncertainty methods for VLA models often require stochastic sampling or supervised labels, limiting their applicability across diverse pretrained models.
llm_ranked.method_en: Proposes a label-free, model-agnostic uncertainty estimation framework based on hidden activation perturbations, motivated by Bayesian perspectives on local model variance.
llm_ranked.result_en: The perturbation-based uncertainty effectively detects failures in VLA models under distribution shift, outperforming sampling-based baselines in failure detection tasks.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: This work provides a practical evaluation tool for foundation model policies, enabling safer deployment of VLA models in robotics.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: Evaluation of foundation models for robot control policies

llm_ranked.evidence_en: VLA framework for autonomous skill acquisition via steerable primitives
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: VLA framework for autonomous skill acquisition via steerable primitives
llm_ranked.tldr_en: InSight addresses the limitation of VLA models that are bounded by skills in training data. It proposes a framework to make VLAs steerable at primitive-action level using an automated segmentation pipeline and a VLM-guided data flywheel for autonomous skill acquisition. Experiments show that steering enables learning novel tasks by composing missing primitives. This work advances generalist robot manipulation by enabling VLAs to autonomously expand their skill repertoires.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: VLA models for manipulation are limited to skills present in their training data, hindering generalization to novel tasks.
llm_ranked.method_en: InSight renders VLAs steerable at primitive-action level via VLM plan decomposition and end-effector pose segmentation, plus a VLM-guided data flywheel for acquiring missing primitives.
llm_ranked.result_en: The framework enables autonomous skill acquisition, allowing robots to learn new manipulation tasks without additional human demonstrations.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Steerable VLAs can self-improve by identifying and practicing missing primitive actions, expanding their capabilities beyond initial demonstrations.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: How can vision language action models be used for generalist robot manipulation?

llm_ranked.evidence_en: Learning embodiment-agnostic action representations from human videos for robot self-improvement
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: Learning embodiment-agnostic action representations from human videos for robot self-improvement
llm_ranked.tldr_en: This paper investigates whether human videos can support robot self-improvement beyond initial policy initialization. The authors learn embodiment-agnostic action, dynamics, and value representations from human videos that transfer across robot embodiments. These representations provide a predictive foundation for robots to evaluate, correct, and improve from their own rollouts. Experiments demonstrate autonomous skill improvement without human intervention. This work shows that passive human video data can fuel a complete robot learning cycle.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Human videos can initialize robot policies but it is unclear if they support autonomous self-improvement through practice and failure.
llm_ranked.method_en: The method learns embodiment-agnostic action, dynamics, and value representations from human videos that transfer across robot embodiments for self-evaluation and correction.
llm_ranked.result_en: Robots using these representations can autonomously improve their own rollouts, demonstrating self-correction and skill acquisition from practice.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Human videos can provide sufficient priors for robots to autonomously improve from their own attempts, enabling a full learning loop.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: imitation learning from human demonstrations for robots

llm_ranked.evidence_en: Generating robot hand designs from human fingertip motion demonstrations
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: Generating robot hand designs from human fingertip motion demonstrations
llm_ranked.tldr_en: This work presents a data-driven framework to generate robot hand designs from human demonstrations. Instead of joint optimization over design and control, it optimizes tree-structured hand designs to reproduce human fingertip motions via inverse kinematics. Using over 4 million frames of human manipulation data, the framework produces a 6-DoF general-purpose hand. This approach decouples design from complex control learning. The results show that human hand motion can directly inform robot hand morphology.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Jointly optimizing robot hand design and control is computationally prohibitive; a data-driven method using human demonstrations is needed.
llm_ranked.method_en: The algorithm optimizes tree-structured robot hands to match human fingertip motion through inverse kinematics, using large-scale human demonstration data.
llm_ranked.result_en: The framework successfully generates a functional 6-DoF general-purpose robot hand from human manipulation data.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Human hand motion data can directly guide the design of robotic hands, simplifying the design process.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: robot manipulation with dexterous hands

llm_ranked.evidence_en: Rotationally equivariant VLA model for generalist robot manipulation
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: Rotationally equivariant VLA model for generalist robot manipulation
llm_ranked.tldr_en: EquiVLA addresses the lack of geometric inductive biases in VLA models for manipulation. It introduces the first end-to-end SO(2)-equivariant VLA framework applicable to any frozen vision-language backbone with a flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head. The framework includes EquiPerceptor for equivariant visual representations and EquiActor for equivariant action generation. Experiments show improved generalization across rotational configurations with less data. This work enhances data efficiency and robustness of VLA manipulation policies.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: VLA models for manipulation lack rotational equivariance, requiring extensive data to generalize across orientations.
llm_ranked.method_en: EquiVLA integrates SO(2)-equivariant representations in both visual perception and action generation within a VLA architecture.
llm_ranked.result_en: The equivariant VLA achieves better generalization to unseen rotations with fewer demonstrations.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Incorporating geometric equivariance improves data efficiency and robustness of VLA models for robot manipulation.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: How can vision language action models be used for generalist robot manipulation?

llm_ranked.evidence_en: Proposes a memory framework for VLA policies in long-horizon manipulation
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: Proposes a memory framework for VLA policies in long-horizon manipulation
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Long-horizon robot manipulation is challenging due to ambiguous observations across stages. KEMO is a lightweight plug-in memory framework that selectively preserves keyframes of task-relevant state changes for VLA policies by combining robot kinematics with visual filtering. It addresses the trade-off between dense history and recent context. Experiments demonstrate improved task completion on long-horizon tasks. This work provides a practical memory augmentation for generalist VLA-based manipulation.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Long-horizon robot manipulation suffers from observation ambiguity that requires task progress inference from execution history.
llm_ranked.method_en: KEMO automatically selects keyframes associated with state changes using robot kinematics and visual filtering for VLA policy memory.
llm_ranked.result_en: KEMO enhances VLA policy performance on long-horizon manipulation tasks by providing selective memory of task-relevant events.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: KEMO offers an effective and lightweight memory plug-in to improve VLA policies for long-horizon generalist robot manipulation.

llm_ranked.evidence_en: Uses generative world models to generate dexterous human hand manipulation data for VLA models
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: Uses generative world models to generate dexterous human hand manipulation data for VLA models
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Scaling dexterous manipulation requires diverse data, but existing sources face trade-offs. Wh0 uses generative video world models to produce egocentric human hand manipulation data conditioned on language, objects, and scenes. It generates a large dataset (WM-H) of 50k episodes to unlock dexterous VLA model capabilities. The approach bridges the sim-to-real gap and embodiment alignment. Results show improved dexterous manipulation on real robots. This contributes directly to robot manipulation with dexterous hands.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Lack of scalable and aligned data for dexterous manipulation limits VLA model performance.
llm_ranked.method_en: Wh0 uses generative world models to synthesize egocentric human hand manipulation videos conditioned on language, objects, and scenes.
llm_ranked.result_en: The generated WM-H dataset enables pretrained dexterous VLA models to perform better on real manipulation tasks.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Wh0 provides a scalable data source for dexterous VLA models, overcoming data alignment challenges.

llm_ranked.evidence_en: egocentric human video for embodied pretraining as imitation learning source
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: egocentric human video for embodied pretraining as imitation learning source
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Embodied foundation models face a data bottleneck due to costly robot data. This paper systematically compares egocentric human video against teleoperated real-robot data for pretraining. The study shows that human video can outperform real-robot data in some settings, offering a scalable and diverse alternative. This finding could reduce reliance on expensive robot data collection and accelerate progress in generalist robotics.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: To address the data bottleneck in embodied pretraining by comparing egocentric human video as a scalable alternative to teleoperated robot data.
llm_ranked.method_en: Systematic study comparing egocentric human video to real-robot data for embodied model pretraining, evaluating performance on downstream tasks.
llm_ranked.result_en: Egocentric human video can outperform real-robot data for embodied pretraining under certain conditions, demonstrating its viability as a data source.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Human video is a promising scalable and diverse data source for embodied foundation models, potentially reducing cost and increasing diversity.

llm_ranked.evidence_en: object-centric residual RL for zero-shot sim-to-real VLA enhancement
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: object-centric residual RL for zero-shot sim-to-real VLA enhancement
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models struggle with precise physical interactions. This paper proposes an object-centric residual reinforcement learning framework that refines VLA actions using object poses, trained purely in simulation. The method enables zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, improving robustness of VLAs on real robots without real-world RL. Results show that the learned corrective policy generalizes across tasks, enhancing VLA performance in precise manipulation.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: To improve the robustness of VLA models in precise physical interactions via residual RL trained in simulation.
llm_ranked.method_en: Object-centric residual RL framework that learns a corrective policy on top of a frozen VLA using object pose supervision in simulation.
llm_ranked.result_en: The residual RL policy enhances VLA robustness zero-shot across real-world manipulation tasks, improving success rates.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Residual RL with object-centric representations enables effective sim-to-real transfer for VLA models without costly real-world RL.

llm_ranked.evidence_en: decoupling declarative from procedural in VLA models for better generalization
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: decoupling declarative from procedural in VLA models for better generalization
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Current VLA models are brittle to spatial and semantic variations despite good in-distribution performance. This work addresses this by decoupling declarative knowledge (object properties) from procedural knowledge (actions). The proposed method separates these components in the VLA architecture, enabling better transfer to novel objects and tasks. Experiments show improved generalization while maintaining in-distribution performance.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: To improve generalization of VLA models by separating declarative and procedural knowledge.
llm_ranked.method_en: Decoupling declarative (object-specific) from procedural (action) knowledge in VLA model architecture.
llm_ranked.result_en: The decoupled VLA model generalizes better to novel objects and tasks without sacrificing in-distribution performance.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Explicit separation of knowledge types enhances VLA transferability and reduces data collection needs.

llm_ranked.evidence_en: zero-shot long-horizon dexterous manipulation using VLM reasoning
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: zero-shot long-horizon dexterous manipulation using VLM reasoning
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Dexterous manipulation and long-horizon tasks remain challenging for robots. This paper presents a zero-shot framework that uses a VLM to produce 2D keypoints from multi-view images, lifted to 3D via multi-view fusion. It supports pick-and-place and tool-use without training. The system demonstrates successful dexterous manipulation in long-horizon tasks, showing that VLMs can ground language into physical actions with geometric reasoning.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: To achieve zero-shot long-horizon dexterous manipulation without end-to-end training.
llm_ranked.method_en: Uses VLM to generate 2D keypoints from multi-view images, then lifts to 3D via triangulation and ray voting for task plan execution.
llm_ranked.result_en: The system performs zero-shot dexterous manipulation on pick-and-place and tool-use tasks in long-horizon scenarios.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: VLMs with multi-view 3D grounding enable zero-shot dexterous manipulation.

llm_ranked.evidence_en: EventVLA: event-driven visual evidence memory for long-horizon VLA policies
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: EventVLA: event-driven visual evidence memory for long-horizon VLA policies
llm_ranked.tldr_en: VLA policies lack memory for long-horizon tasks when visual cues are occluded. EventVLA introduces an end-to-end framework with sparse visual evidence memory including foundational visual anchors and a dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory module. This selectively stores keyframes to avoid bottlenecks and redundancy. Experiments show improved success on long-horizon manipulation tasks by maintaining relevant visual context over time, enabling robust policy execution.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: To address memory limitations in VLA policies for long-horizon robotic manipulation.
llm_ranked.method_en: EventVLA with foundational visual anchors and dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory for sparse visual memory.
llm_ranked.result_en: EventVLA improves long-horizon manipulation success by retaining relevant visual evidence.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Sparse visual memory enhances VLA policy robustness for long-horizon tasks.

llm_ranked.evidence_en: geometric inductive bias for vision-language-action models in generalist manipulation
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: geometric inductive bias for vision-language-action models in generalist manipulation
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have advanced generalist robot manipulation but lack geometric grounding from camera calibration. G^3VLA injects calibrated structure into the visual-token stream via intrinsic-conditioned ray embeddings, projective positional encoding, and bidirectional cross-view fusion. Experiments show improved manipulation performance, especially in multi-camera setups, without altering the action space or imitation objective. This work demonstrates the importance of geometric inductive bias for VLA models.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: VLA models process visual tokens in 2D coordinates without camera geometry, causing issues in multi-camera setups.
llm_ranked.method_en: G^3VLA adds a camera-aware geometric module with ray embeddings, projective positional encoding, and cross-view fusion to pretrained VLA.
llm_ranked.result_en: Improved performance on manipulation tasks with multi-camera setups without retraining the action space.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Geometric inductive bias is crucial for VLA models to achieve robust generalist manipulation in real-world multi-camera environments.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: vision language action model for robot control

llm_ranked.evidence_en: cross-robot data scaling for generalist policies using Cartesian state delta representation
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: cross-robot data scaling for generalist policies using Cartesian state delta representation
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Robot learning from diverse embodiments faces action representation mismatch. SPACE proposes using Cartesian state delta as a universal action representation and introduces a framework with three-level handling of robot dynamics variation. This enables learning from cross-robot demonstrations and deploying on different robots, advancing toward generalist policies. Evaluated across multiple robot platforms, SPACE shows improved generalization and cross-robot transfer.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Different robots require different actions for the same motion, hindering cross-robot policy learning.
llm_ranked.method_en: SPACE uses Cartesian state delta as universal action representation and adapts at state prediction and command execution levels.
llm_ranked.result_en: Enables effective learning and deployment across diverse robot platforms, improving generalization.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Unified action representation is key to scaling robot learning across embodiments for generalist policies.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: large scale robot learning with diverse data

llm_ranked.evidence_en: geometry-guided VLA adaptation from synthetic videos
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: geometry-guided VLA adaptation from synthetic videos
llm_ranked.tldr_en: VLA models require large-scale video-action pairs, but real teleoperation is scarce. Synthetic robot videos offer scalable alternative, but deriving low-level control from generated visuals is problematic due to loss of control signals. Proposes geometry-guided VLA adaptation that uses only surviving geometric information from synthetic videos. Adapts VLA model using geometry instead of pseudo-actions, improving real-world performance.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Scarcity of real teleoperation data limits VLA model training.
llm_ranked.method_en: Proposes geometry-guided adaptation from synthetic robot videos, avoiding pseudo-action recovery.
llm_ranked.result_en: Improves VLA model performance on real robot tasks compared to pseudo-action baselines.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Leveraging geometry from synthetic videos is a viable data augmentation for VLA models.

llm_ranked.evidence_en: reconstructing and retargeting human videos to dexterous robotic hands
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: reconstructing and retargeting human videos to dexterous robotic hands
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Scalable data generation for dexterous robotic hand manipulation from human videos. DO AS I DO reconstructs hand-object interactions from monocular RGB videos and retargets to multi-fingered robotic hands. Addresses hand-object interaction estimation and embodiment gap. Enables use of abundant human videos as robot manipulation data.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Scarcity of data for dexterous robotic hand manipulation.
llm_ranked.method_en: DO AS I DO reconstructs and retargets human hand-object interactions from RGB videos to robot hands.
llm_ranked.result_en: Successfully generates dexterous manipulation data from various in-the-wild video sources.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: Human videos can be a scalable source for dexterous robot manipulation data.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: robot manipulation with dexterous hands

llm_ranked.evidence_en: failure detection framework for vision-language-action models
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: failure detection framework for vision-language-action models
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) can behave unpredictably in out-of-distribution scenarios. VLA-FAIL proposes a lightweight failure detection framework combining last-layer Mahalanobis distance and an energy-based detector, requiring no failure data. Experiments show it effectively detects failures across various VLA finetuned models with minimal overhead. This enables safer real-world deployment of VLA models.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Runtime failure detection is essential for safe deployment of VLAs, but existing methods are computationally expensive.
llm_ranked.method_en: VLA-FAIL combines LLMD and energy-based detection to detect failures in VLA models without failure data.
llm_ranked.result_en: The method achieves efficient and accurate failure detection across multiple finetuned VLA models.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: VLA-FAIL provides a lightweight, broadly applicable failure detection framework for VLAs.
llm_ranked.matched_query_text: vision language action model for robot control

llm_ranked.evidence_en: RL-based data generation for dexterous bimanual manipulation
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: RL-based data generation for dexterous bimanual manipulation
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Training generalist policies for bimanual dexterous manipulation requires large-scale datasets. This work proposes a systematic RL-based data generation framework that uses task rewards and diverse initialization to produce high-quality trajectories. It overcomes the limitations of human teleoperation and object-centric matching. Experiments show that policies trained on this synthetic data achieve strong performance on multiple language-conditioned bimanual tasks.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Lack of large-scale high-quality datasets is a bottleneck for bimanual dexterous manipulation learning.
llm_ranked.method_en: A systematic RL-based data generation framework using task rewards and diverse initial states.
llm_ranked.result_en: Policies trained on synthetic data achieve strong performance on language-conditioned bimanual tasks.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: This work provides a scalable data generation alternative to human demonstrations for bimanual manipulation.

llm_ranked.evidence_en: coordination-aware VLA for dual-arm manipulation
llm_ranked.canonical_evidence: coordination-aware VLA for dual-arm manipulation
llm_ranked.tldr_en: Bimanual manipulation with VLA models often lacks explicit coordination. Co-VLA introduces structured priors into the action head for coordinated dual-arm control. It replaces the monolithic action head with a structured module that models inter-arm dependencies. Experiments show improved reliability and stability on tightly coupled bimanual tasks. This provides a principled way to incorporate coordination into VLA models.
llm_ranked.motivation_en: Implicit coordination in VLA models is insufficient for tightly coupled bimanual tasks.
llm_ranked.method_en: Co-VLA replaces the action head with a structured module that models inter-arm dependencies.
llm_ranked.result_en: The method improves reliability and stability on tightly coupled bimanual manipulation tasks.
llm_ranked.conclusion_en: This work shows that explicit coordination priors enhance VLA model performance in bimanual settings.
